narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated a 49.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and avoid high failure risks. This prediction hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA being the most influential, as it strongly pushed the model toward expecting you to pass by showcasing your academic strength. However, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average of 5.53—had the next biggest impact, nudging the prediction toward failure because it signals weaker performance relative to peers at that stage. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 2, compared to the average of 5.71, contributed toward the failure risk by highlighting early struggles in your school's grading context. Other details like your middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 31 (below the 36.7 average), and full-time student status played lesser roles, with full-time being common among successful applicants. In contrast to typical students who pass the bar, you ranked lower in those critical school deciles, which often correlate with bar success more than undergrad grades alone. Your stronger undergrad performance helped offset some concerns, but not enough to tip the scales. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average and addressing those decile rankings, perhaps through post-baccalaureate courses or explaining any extenuating circumstances in a new application. Strengthening early-grade indicators could make a big difference next time, and schools value persistence. You have the undergrad foundation to build on—keep pushing.
